The Observatory

Two telescopes. One facing out. One facing in.
The building that sees furthest should also see most honestly.

The village at a glance
When residents reaches 150 and villages in the network reaches 2 — the architecture has proven itself.
The Roundtable lens
The Roundtable is a lens. It shows you what you can't see with the naked eye — the patterns, the contradictions, the gap between stated values and actual behaviour.
Telescope 2 — Facing out
Where the village reaches
Villages on the horizon
Clay Village is a proof of concept. These are the communities waiting to exist.

The Long View
The founder speaking to the community. Not a feed. A scroll. Added to deliberately, not constantly.

The future belongs to people who make with their hands.
The Mentor Cluster

The Library

What makers know. Written down.
Journals, process notes, glaze recipes.

Not posts. Not captions. Things worth writing down — about the work, the process, and why any of it matters.

Add a journal entry

Technical knowledge from real practice. How things were made, what went wrong, what was learned. The kind of knowledge that used to die with the maker.

Add a process note

Member-contributed recipes. Every firing is an experiment. Search by cone, colour, or surface.

Submit a recipe
Every maker who was invisible. Their work lives here.
The Gallery

Ceramic Market
Every maker, one place. No algorithm. No likes.

Pieces for sale
Ordered by craft age — the maker who has practised longest appears first. Never by engagement.
Add a piece to the market
Every maker. One place. No algorithm.
The Bonfire
THE SYSTEM

Instagram is the world's biggest art gallery and craft marketplace. But it was never built for artists and makers. It was built for engagement. For advertising revenue. For keeping people scrolling.

The uncomfortable truth: Instagram needs makers more than makers need Instagram.

81% of creators use it to market their work. It has become the default channel for makers -- not because it's the best tool for them, but because there was no alternative. They didn't choose it consciously. They followed where everyone else went. And then got trapped by the sunk cost of everything they built there.

The platform runs on hope. And hope is the product.

THE CASINO

77% of all Instagram influencer accounts are nano-influencers -- people with 1,000 to 10,000 followers.

The vast majority of makers are grinding in that zone. Posting consistently. Learning the algorithm. Chasing engagement. Not because it's working. Because the platform constantly shows them the few who made it.

This is exactly how a casino operates. Everyone sees the person winning. Nobody sees the thousands who didn't.

The dream of growth is the product. Instagram's business model needs millions of small creators producing free content. The dream keeps them producing.

A potter with 3,000 loyal people who actually buy her work -- who commission pieces, come to workshops, recommend to friends -- feels like a failure next to someone with 50,000 followers who sells nothing tangible.

The platform measures the wrong things for makers. And makers have internalized those wrong measurements as truth.

They don't know it. That is the core problem. They accept how the situation is. They are made to believe that everyone can be successful if they just work harder.

But just a few get there. The system is designed that way.

"You earn the reach, but the algorithm keeps the relationship."

A maker can build 10,000 followers through genuine work. The platform owns those relationships. It can reassign their audience to someone else tomorrow. The maker has no home. Just a borrowed crowd.

Clay Village gives the relationship back to the maker.

THE LIE

People with more followers have perceived status. More say. More authority. Even when that authority is completely disconnected from actual mastery.

The 50k account knows this. They have reach. People give them authority based on that number. So they think that way too -- they've internalized the metric even when they know it's hollow.

Meanwhile the real masters -- people with 20 years of practice, extraordinary skill, deep knowledge -- have no social media following because they were too busy actually mastering their craft.

They know the truth about quality. But they have no platform to speak from. They are frustrated. And invisible.

You cannot just remove the metric. You have to replace it with a better one.

THE VASE

When you hold a well-made vase and it's balanced -- then you know.

No algorithm can replicate that moment. No reel, no photo, no follower count can tell you what your hands know in three seconds.

Clay Village doesn't replace that embodied moment of knowing. It creates enough trust that the visitor gets on a plane, drives to the studio, attends the residency, holds the vase.

The platform is the bridge to the real world. Not the replacement for it.

THE TRAP

We are approaching post-labour economics.

The grinding. The escaping. The climbing. And then you turn around and your life is over.

People who philosophize about life in a post-labour economy get scared of depression without work. Because that's who we've become -- people who derive purpose entirely from productivity.

The trap: we can't figure out what life is actually for.

Making things. Being present. Genuine connection. But by the time we understand that, will the window have already closed?

Clay Village sits inside this tension. It doesn't solve it. But it points toward something -- that the making itself is the point. Not the metrics around the making.

THE VILLAGE

You can't just remove the metric. You have to replace it with a better one.

In a real village, authority was earned through:

Time in the craft -- how long have they been doing this seriously.
Vouching -- who in the community respects them and says so.
Teaching -- did they pass knowledge on, did students grow.
Real transactions -- did people actually pay, return, commission again.
Peer recognition -- do other serious makers consider them serious.

None of these can be bought. None of them can go viral. They take years.

That's the point.

THE ARK

The flood is the algorithm. AI-generated slop drowning real craft. Follower counts replacing mastery. Makers grinding content instead of making things.

We are building the Ark.

Not because we think Instagram will disappear. Because we think the real ones deserve a place that was designed to see them.

The potter with 3,000 deeply loyal people is more valuable here than the account with 50,000 who buys nothing and makes noise.

The master with no following and 30 years of knowledge in their hands -- they are visible here. Finally.

THE PROMISE

Clay Village measures different things.

How long you've been doing this.
Whether your students went on to make real work.
Whether collectors come back.
Whether other serious makers consider you serious.

These things can't be bought. They can't go viral. They take years.

That's the point.

The people who disagree most violently about what this place should be actually want the same thing — respect for the work. They just define respect differently.

Stop counting followers.
Start counting what counts.

What do you believe? Why do you make?
This is where the truth gets said.
🌿 Residency & Exchange

Studio swaps. Visiting residencies. Community credits.
Real connections between potters, not listings on a platform.

Post a listing

No certificates. No streaks. Just the work.

AnotherClay (Ben) · 3 years · Chiang Mai
First Contact with Clay

Four hours at the wheel. You'll centre, open, pull walls, and fail beautifully. The same workshop that started everything for the teacher — now taught. All materials. Pieces fired and shipped.

Beginner In person Chiang Mai 4 hrs
€ 85

AnotherClay (Ben) · 3 years · Location open
Tall Forms — Finding Your Voice

A full day on proportion in tall throwing. Where the belly sits, how the neck breathes, why the foot matters. We throw, critique, and throw again. Lunch included.

Intermediate In person Full day Max 6
€ 220
Notify me →
AnotherClay (Ben) · 3 years · Online
Glazing Without Fear

Two hours online on glaze as a material with its own logic — surface tension, temperature, layering, how to read a failed firing. Recording included for 30 days.

Online All levels 2 hrs Recording
€ 45

Want to teach here?

The School is for real makers who teach what they know. If you have a skill and want to run a workshop — in person or online — you can list it here.

Curated sequences of workshops and skills — from first contact with clay to throwing your first tall vase. Built by experienced makers, not algorithms.

We teach the hand. The algorithm teaches nothing.
